skill.name: aws-compliance-checker
skill.description: Review AWS account settings against CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 using AWS CLI and boto3 examples.
skill.summary: Checks AWS security posture against common compliance frameworks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The high-severity static findings are false positives caused by compliance terminology, public CIDR test values, and references to weak configurations. The skill contains executable AWS CLI and boto3 examples that enumerate account resources and write a local report, so it should be published with a warning to use read-only scoped credentials.

medium_findings.title: AWS Account Enumeration Through Executable Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill provides bash and Python examples that call AWS CLI and boto3 to enumerate IAM users, CloudTrail, S3 buckets, VPCs, security groups, EBS volumes, RDS instances, and CloudWatch Logs. This is expected for a compliance checker, but it should only run with approved read-only credentials in the intended AWS account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and boto3 clients are plainly visible in the cited examples. The intent is legitimate compliance inspection, so the risk is operational exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Compliance Report May Contain Sensitive Findings
medium_findings.description: The report generator writes compliance results to a dated local JSON file. The file may contain account identifiers, resource names, and security weaknesses, so users should protect output storage and avoid committing reports.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and the report content is built from compliance checks. Sensitivity depends on the account data returned at runtime.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Public CIDR Values Are Compliance Test Inputs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded 0.0.0.0/0 strings are used to detect insecure security group exposure for SSH, RDP, and PCI network checks. I did not find evidence that these values create network listeners or outbound connections.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines compare AWS security group data against public CIDR strings. The surrounding text labels these as compliance checks for open ingress.

low_findings.title: External URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to CIS and AWS documentation pages in the Additional Resources section. I did not find evidence of programmatic network requests to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as Markdown links. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or data transmission logic tied to them.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Keywords Are Compliance Terminology
low_findings.description: The C2 and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from terms such as flow logs, key age, default security groups, encryption checks, and compliance framework names. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior, cryptographic implementation, or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear inside defensive audit logic. The file contains no override instructions, hidden payloads, encryption routines, or remote control endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Pipelines Execute AWS CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple Markdown examples invoke aws, jq, awk, grep, and shell command substitution. These examples are useful for audits, but they execute live account queries when copied or run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell command patterns are explicit. The commands are read-oriented AWS compliance checks, so they are risky only if run with inappropriate credentials or in the wrong environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python boto3 Examples Query AWS Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: The PCI and reporting examples use boto3 clients to query EC2, RDS, S3, IAM, and CloudTrail data. This can expose cloud inventory and compliance gaps in generated output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The boto3 client calls are directly shown in the examples. No exfiltration endpoint is present, but returned inventory can be sensitive.

content.value_statement: AWS audit preparation requires repeatable checks across identity, logging, networking, and encryption. This skill provides framework-focused examples for CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 review.
actual_capabilities: Provides AWS CLI examples for CIS IAM, logging, monitoring, and networking checks.
Shows boto3 examples for PCI-DSS resource and access control review.
Includes HIPAA-focused checks for MFA, CloudTrail, EBS encryption, and RDS encryption.
Outlines a basic compliance report generator with framework selection.
Links checks to common frameworks, including CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2.
limitations: Requires valid AWS credentials and installed command-line tools before examples can run.
Does not provide complete coverage for every control in each compliance framework.
Does not replace formal audit evidence review or legal compliance advice.
Some report helper functions are placeholders and need implementation before production use.

use_cases.title: Prepare for a Cloud Security Audit
use_cases.description: Run repeatable checks for IAM, logging, network exposure, and encryption before an external assessment.

use_cases.title: Map AWS Findings to Compliance Frameworks
use_cases.description: Review AWS resources against CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, or SOC 2 control areas for gap tracking.

use_cases.title: Build Internal Compliance Reporting
use_cases.description: Adapt the report examples into a scheduled workflow that summarizes pass and fail counts.

prompt_templates.title: Start a CIS Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-compliance-checker to review my AWS account against CIS AWS Foundations. Focus on IAM, logging, and networking controls.

prompt_templates.title: Check PCI-DSS Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a PCI-DSS AWS compliance checklist for EC2, RDS, S3, IAM, CloudTrail, and encryption controls.

prompt_templates.title: Create a HIPAA Evidence Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me assess HIPAA technical safeguards in AWS. Include access controls, audit controls, encryption at rest, and transmission security.

prompt_templates.title: Design Continuous Compliance Reporting
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt this skill into a weekly AWS compliance report workflow with read-only permissions, protected output storage, and exception tracking.

output_examples.input: Run a CIS AWS Foundations review for IAM and logging.
output_examples.output: A checklist of root MFA, unused credentials, password policy, CloudTrail status, AWS Config status, and S3 logging findings.

output_examples.input: Summarize PCI-DSS gaps in my AWS account.
output: Network security: identify public security group exposure.
Data protection: list unencrypted volumes that need remediation.
Access control: identify IAM users without MFA.

output_examples.input: Prepare an audit report for HIPAA controls.
output_examples.output: A framework-specific summary covering MFA, CloudTrail, storage encryption, database encryption, and transmission security follow-up items.

best_practices: Run examples with least-privilege, read-only AWS credentials in the intended account.
Store generated reports in protected locations because they may reveal security gaps.
Validate results with AWS Config, Security Hub, and human review before audit submission.
anti_patterns: Do not run the examples with administrator credentials unless there is an approved operational reason.
Do not treat sample output as complete evidence for formal compliance certification.
Do not commit generated compliance reports or account inventory to public repositories.

faq.question: Does this skill change AWS resources?
faq.answer: The documented examples are primarily read-only checks. Users should still review commands before running them in any AWS account.

faq.question: Which compliance frameworks are covered?
faq.answer: The skill references CIS AWS Foundations, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 control areas.

faq.question: Can it prove compliance by itself?
faq.answer: No. It helps find gaps and organize evidence, but formal compliance requires full control review and auditor judgment.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The examples rely on AWS CLI, jq, awk, grep, shell access, Python, and boto3 depending on the selected check.

faq.question: Why is there a medium security rating?
faq.answer: The skill includes executable commands that inspect AWS account inventory and can write sensitive compliance reports to disk.

faq.question: How should teams run it safely?
faq.answer: Use scoped read-only credentials, run in approved accounts, protect output files, and review commands before execution.
